section.title: Community Laws
section.sectionRationale: This chapter fences the worshipping community and the camp with holiness: who may join the assembly, purity in the camp where the LORD walks, protection for a runaway slave, a ban on cultic prostitution, honest lending among brothers, and the seriousness of a vow, showing that covenant holiness touches worship, camp life, economics, and everyday speech alike.
section.thesis: Because the LORD Himself walks among His people, this chapter protects the purity of Israel's worshipping assembly and camp, extends unusual mercy to a runaway slave and the poor brother needing an interest-free loan, and insists that a vow, once spoken to the LORD, must be kept.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: who may and may not enter the assembly of the LORD; the purity of the camp, since the LORD walks there; protection for a slave who has taken refuge; the ban on shrine prostitution and its wages; the ban on charging a brother interest; the requirement to keep a vow once made; and the right to eat, but not harvest, from a neighbor's field.

verses.text: No man with crushed or severed genitals may enter the assembly of the LORD.
No one of illegitimate birth may enter the assembly of the LORD, nor may any of his descendants, even to the tenth generation.
No Ammonite or Moabite or any of their descendants may enter the assembly of the LORD, even to the tenth generation.
For they did not meet you with food and water on your way out of Egypt, and they hired Balaam son of Beor from Pethor in Aram-naharaim to curse you.
Yet the LORD your God would not listen to Balaam, and the LORD your God turned the curse into a blessing for you, because the LORD your God loves you.
You are not to seek peace or prosperity from them as long as you live.
Do not despise an Edomite, for he is your brother. Do not despise an Egyptian, because you lived as a foreigner in his land.
The third generation of children born to them may enter the assembly of the LORD.
When you are encamped against your enemies, then you shall keep yourself from every wicked thing.
If any man among you becomes unclean because of a nocturnal emission, he must leave the camp and stay outside.
When evening approaches, he must wash with water, and when the sun sets he may return to the camp.
You must have a place outside the camp to go and relieve yourself.
And you must have a digging tool in your equipment so that when you relieve yourself you can dig a hole and cover up your excrement.
For the LORD your God walks throughout your camp to protect you and deliver your enemies to you. Your camp must be holy, lest He see anything unclean among you and turn away from you.

units.label: A refuge for the runaway slave

verses.text: Do not return a slave to his master if he has taken refuge with you.
Let him live among you wherever he chooses, in the town of his pleasing. Do not oppress him.
No daughter or son of Israel is to be a {{qedeshah|shrine prostitute}}.
You must not bring the wages of a prostitute, whether female or male, into the house of the LORD your God to fulfill any vow, because both are detestable to the LORD your God.

units.label: No interest on a brother's loan

verses.text: Do not charge your brother {{neshekh|interest}} on money, food, or any other type of loan.
You may charge a foreigner interest, but not your brother, so that the LORD your God may bless you in everything to which you put your hand in the land that you are entering to possess.

units.label: Keep your vow
units.note: optional to make, required to keep

verses.text: If you make a {{neder|vow}} to the LORD your God, do not be slow to keep it, because He will surely require it of you, and you will be guilty of sin.
But if you refrain from making a vow, you will not be guilty of sin.
Be careful to follow through on what comes from your lips, because you have freely vowed to the LORD your God with your own mouth.
When you enter your neighbor's vineyard, you may eat your fill of grapes, but you must not put any in your basket.
When you enter your neighbor's grainfield, you may pluck the heads of grain with your hand, but you must not put a sickle to your neighbor's grain.

qedeshah.gloss: Shrine prostitute, literally built on the Hebrew word for 'holy' or 'set apart.' Canaanite fertility religion twisted the language of holiness into cultic sex; Israel's own worship was to have absolutely nothing to do with it.

neshekh.gloss: Interest, literally a 'bite,' from a root meaning to bite. Charging interest to a needy brother was forbidden, since the loan was meant as an act of covenant kindness, not a source of profit.

neder.gloss: Vow, a freely made promise to the LORD. Making a vow was entirely optional, but once spoken it had to be kept fully and without delay.

basic.context: This chapter sets boundaries for who may join Israel's worshipping assembly, requires the war camp to be kept ritually clean because the LORD Himself walks there, protects a runaway slave from being returned to bondage, forbids the cultic prostitution common in Canaanite religion, bans charging interest to a needy brother, and insists that a vow, once made, must be kept exactly.
basic.hermeneutics: These specific exclusions, purity rules, and economic laws describe Israel's particular old covenant community. Some, like the assembly exclusions, are later reversed in principle by the gospel's welcome of the nations and the eunuch (Isaiah 56:3-5; Acts 8:26-39), while the underlying concerns, protecting worship's integrity, honest speech, and generosity to the poor, remain.
basic.originalLanguages: Israel's own worship must never adopt the qedeshah, shrine prostitute, of Canaanite religion, a term ironically built on the word for 'holy.' Loans to a needy brother must carry no neshekh, interest, literally a 'bite.' And a neder, vow, once spoken to the LORD, must be kept fully.
basic.typology: The runaway slave who finds refuge and must not be returned to his master pictures the gospel's welcome of every fugitive from sin who flees to God for shelter, rather than being sent back into bondage.
basic.christ: Isaiah 56:3-5 promises that the very eunuch excluded from the assembly in this chapter will one day be given 'a name better than sons and daughters' in God's house, a promise fulfilled when Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch in Acts 8:26-39, the gospel deliberately reversing this chapter's exclusion in Christ.
basic.god: God cares that His worshipping community remains pure and honest, yet He is also the God who, in time, welcomes the outsider, the eunuch, the foreigner, the very categories fenced out here, showing His ultimate purpose was always to widen the gate through the gospel.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 56:3-5 promises inclusion for the eunuch, reversing this chapter's exclusion. Acts 8:26-39 shows Philip baptizing the Ethiopian eunuch, fulfilling that promise. Ruth, a Moabite, becomes an ancestress of David and Christ despite this chapter's exclusion of Moabites. Galatians 3:28 says in Christ there is neither slave nor free.
basic.oneStory: The old covenant fenced the assembly and the camp with real boundaries, yet even within the Old Testament, God's grace to Ruth the Moabite hints at where the story is heading: the gospel's welcome of every nation, the eunuch, and the outsider into the one people of God in Christ.

context.literary: This chapter continues the collection of practical case laws following chapters 21-22, moving from family and property law to the boundaries of the worshipping assembly and the camp.
context.historical: Ammon and Moab's hostility during the exodus (verse 4) and the fertility cult prostitution common among Canaanite religions form the immediate background for these specific exclusions and prohibitions.
context.canonical: Ruth the Moabite's inclusion in Israel and in the messianic line, and Isaiah's later promise to the eunuch, show the Old Testament itself anticipating the gospel's wider welcome that this chapter's boundaries would one day give way to.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To protect the purity and integrity of Israel's worshipping assembly, camp, and economic life among brothers, in response to Israel's specific history with Ammon, Moab, and pagan cultic practice.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific exclusions from the assembly and camp purity laws describe Israel's particular old covenant community and historical grievances; they are not directly prescriptive for the church, which the New Testament explicitly opens to the very categories excluded here. The prohibitions on cultic prostitution, on charging the poor predatory interest, and on breaking a vow remain enduring moral concerns.

debates.question: How should Christians read the assembly exclusions of verses 1-8 in light of Isaiah 56 and Acts 8?

views.label: As old covenant boundaries later reversed in Christ
views.case: Isaiah 56:3-5 explicitly promises the eunuch inclusion, and Acts 8:26-39 shows this fulfilled; the assembly exclusions were bound to Israel's specific covenant community and its history, not a permanent statement about who God ultimately welcomes.

views.label: As boundaries protecting covenant identity in their moment
views.case: In their own historical setting these laws protected Israel's distinct identity against nations that had shown hostility or represented corrupt religion, functioning similarly to membership boundaries protecting a community's integrity, without implying anything final about salvation.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the boundaries were for that specific covenant community and time, and that the gospel deliberately and explicitly widens the welcome to the very people once excluded.

study.originalLanguages: Israel's own worship must never adopt the qedeshah, shrine prostitute, verse 17, of Canaanite religion, a term ironically built on the word for holy. Loans to a needy brother must carry no neshekh, interest, verse 19, and a neder, vow, verse 21, once spoken to the LORD, must be kept fully.

typology.type: The runaway slave granted refuge rather than returned to bondage
typology.fulfillment: Every sinner who flees to Christ finds welcome rather than being sent back into slavery to sin, Romans 6:17-18.

typology.type: The eunuch excluded from the assembly
typology.fulfillment: Isaiah 56:3-5 promises him inclusion, fulfilled when the Ethiopian eunuch is baptized in Acts 8:26-39.

study.christ: Isaiah 56:3-5 promises that the eunuch excluded from the assembly in this chapter will be given 'a name better than sons and daughters' in God's house, a promise fulfilled when Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch in Acts 8:26-39, the gospel deliberately reversing this chapter's exclusion in Christ, in whom there is neither slave nor free, Galatians 3:28.
study.god: God cares that His worshipping community remains pure and honest, yet He is also the God who, in time, welcomes the outsider, the eunuch, the foreigner, the very categories fenced out here, showing His ultimate purpose was always to widen the gate through the gospel.

crossReferences.note: Promises inclusion for the eunuch, reversing this chapter's exclusion.
Philip baptizes the Ethiopian eunuch, fulfilling that promise.
Ruth, a Moabite, becomes an ancestress of David and Christ despite this chapter's exclusion of Moabites.
In Christ there is neither slave nor free.

study.oneStory: The old covenant fenced the assembly and the camp with real boundaries, yet even within the Old Testament, God's grace to Ruth the Moabite hints at where the story is heading: the gospel's welcome of every nation, the eunuch, and the outsider into the one people of God in Christ.

quiz.q: Why were Ammonites and Moabites excluded from the assembly of the LORD, according to verse 4?

options.text: Because they did not meet Israel with food and water on the journey from Egypt, and hired Balaam to curse Israel.
options.note: Verse 4 states this explicit historical reason.

options.text: Because they worshiped on a different calendar.
options.note: The stated reason is their hostility during the exodus, not calendar differences.

options.text: Because they were too numerous.
options.note: The stated reason is historical hostility, not population size.

options.text: No reason is given for their exclusion.
options.note: Verse 4 gives an explicit historical reason.

quiz.q: What does the law say about a slave who has escaped and taken refuge with you?

options.text: Do not return him to his master; let him live among you wherever he chooses, without oppression.
options.note: Verses 15-16 state this explicitly.

options.text: You must return him immediately to avoid punishment.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids returning him.

options.text: You must sell him to someone else.
options.note: The text commands protection, not resale.

options.text: The law says nothing about this situation.
options.note: Verses 15-16 give explicit instruction.

quiz.q: What does the law say about charging interest on a loan to a fellow Israelite?

options.text: It is forbidden; you may charge a foreigner interest, but not your brother.
options.note: Verses 19-20 state this explicit distinction.

options.text: Interest may be charged to anyone at any rate.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids interest to a fellow Israelite.

options.text: Interest is required on every loan.
options.note: The text forbids, rather than requires, interest among brothers.

options.text: The law does not address lending at all.
options.note: Verses 19-20 address it directly.

quiz.q: According to verses 21-23, what should a person do about a vow made to the LORD?

options.text: Keep it without delay, since God will require it, though it was never sinful to have refrained from vowing in the first place.
options.note: Verses 21-23 state this explicit principle.

options.text: Vows may be broken freely with no consequence.
options.note: Verse 21 explicitly warns that God will require it and count delay as sin.

options.text: Everyone is required to make vows regularly.
options.note: Verse 22 explicitly says refraining from a vow is not sinful.

options.text: The chapter has nothing to say about vows.
options.note: Verses 21-23 address vows directly.

quiz.q: What does the law permit when passing through a neighbor's vineyard or grainfield?

options.text: You may eat your fill by hand, but you must not harvest it with a basket or sickle to take away.
options.note: Verses 24-25 state this exact permission and limit.

options.text: You may harvest as much as you can carry to sell.
options.note: The text explicitly forbids using a basket or sickle to harvest for taking away.

options.text: You may not touch any of it under any circumstance.
options.note: The text explicitly permits eating your fill by hand.

options.text: The law is silent on this situation.
options.note: Verses 24-25 address it explicitly.

questions: This chapter protected a runaway slave rather than returning him to bondage. Who in your life has fled from a harmful situation, and how can you offer them the same kind of refuge rather than sending them back?
Israel was forbidden from charging a poor brother interest, since the loan was meant as covenant kindness, not profit. Where might your own generosity be quietly conditional on what you get back?
A vow to the LORD had to be kept exactly, even though making one was never required. Where have you made promises to God, or to others, that you have been slow to keep?
The eunuch was excluded here, yet Isaiah promised, and Acts fulfilled, his full welcome in God's house. Who do you find it hard to imagine God fully welcoming, and how does the gospel challenge that?
You were allowed to eat your fill from a neighbor's field but not to exploit it. Where is the line between enjoying what God provides through others' generosity and taking advantage of it?

translationNotes.note: The specific exclusions here concern eligibility for particular roles or full participation in Israel's covenant assembly under the old covenant; Isaiah 56:3-8 and Acts 8:26-39 show these very categories, the eunuch and the foreigner, explicitly welcomed under the gospel.

'Shrine prostitute' translates qedeshah, ironically built on the Hebrew word for 'holy'; Canaanite fertility religion twisted the language of holiness into cultic sex, which Israel's worship was to have nothing to do with.

The permission to charge foreigners interest but not brothers reflects the loan's purpose among Israelites as an act of covenant generosity to the poor, not commercial profit; it is not a general principle about how Israel was to treat foreigners in every matter.
